name: Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH)

x.invariant: a tree of nested bounding volumes (usually AABBs); a parent’s volume encloses all of its children’s primitives.
x.memoryOverhead: Two child pointers + a bounding box per node; ~2n nodes for n leaves.
x.locality: Linearised (flattened) BVHs are traversed front-to-back for good cache behaviour on GPUs/CPUs.
x.mutationModel: Built then queried; dynamic scenes refit or rebuild per frame.
x.concurrency: Read-only queries parallelise trivially; parallel build (e.g. LBVH) is common on GPUs.

def: A tree of nested bounding volumes used to prune spatial queries — the standard acceleration structure for ray tracing and collision broad-phase.
problem: Avoid testing a ray or moving object against every primitive in a large scene.
mechanism: Recursively groups primitives into bounding volumes (often AABBs); a query descends only into volumes it intersects, skipping whole subtrees. Build quality (e.g. SAH) trades build time for query speed.
limits: Rebuild needed for large scene changes
failures: Stale bounds after un-refitted motion → missed hits
whenNot: Uniform grids fit the data better (use a grid / k-d tree)
Static 2D point queries (use a k-d tree / quadtree)
myths: "BVH and k-d tree are interchangeable" — BVHs bound objects; k-d trees partition space
